quick_facts.label: Occupational area
quick_facts.value: Food and Beverage Service Executive is a hospitality-service role centred on preparing service areas, receiving guests, supporting or delivering food and beverage service, clearing tables, and maintaining service, hygiene, and safety standards.

quick_facts.label: Common work settings
quick_facts.value: Relevant food-and-beverage outlets include specialty restaurants, coffee shops/all-day dining, cafeterias, quick-service restaurants, grills, banquets, bars and pubs; the wider diploma syllabus also covers room service, outdoor catering, airline, hospital, cruise-line and railway catering.

quick_facts.label: Degree route
quick_facts.value: NCHMCT’s B.Sc. in Hospitality and Hotel Administration is a three-year, six-semester programme with laboratory training in Food & Beverage Service; admissions are through NCHM JEE and central counselling for participating institutes.

quick_facts.label: Focused diploma route
quick_facts.value: NCHMCT lists a Diploma in Food & Beverage Service of 1.5 years. Its published eligibility is 10+2 or equivalent with English as a subject, and its structure includes teaching plus 24 weeks of industrial training.

quick_facts.label: Indicative progression framework
quick_facts.value: In the Tourism & Hospitality Skill Council’s published pathway, Food & Beverage Service Assistant can progress to Food & Beverage Service Associate, Food & Beverage Supervisor, Restaurant Captain, Restaurant Manager, Manager–Banquet Operations, and Food & Beverage Service Manager. Actual titles and promotion criteria vary by employer.

sections.heading: What the role does
paragraphs: A Food and Beverage Service Executive helps deliver a smooth guest dining experience before, during, and after service. In practical terms, this includes checking the cleanliness and layout of tables and chairs, setting linen, tableware and glassware, stocking side stations, greeting guests, accommodating seating or special needs, coordinating with kitchen and service colleagues, clearing used ware, and resetting tables for the next guests.

The exact seniority attached to “Executive” is employer-specific. The nationally documented THSC service-role standards are useful as a baseline for operational service work, while individual hotels, restaurants and banquet operations may add order-taking, billing, inventory, staff-coordination, or shift-opening and closing duties.
bullets: Complete mise-en-place: tables, linen, menus, cutlery, crockery, glassware, condiments and service stations.
Welcome and assist guests professionally, including seating adjustments and special requirements.
Coordinate with kitchen, management and service staff to keep service timely and orderly.
Clear, reset and inspect service areas; follow workplace hygiene, safety, privacy and service procedures.

sections.heading: Education routes in India
paragraphs: The immediate parent programmes provide three practical routes into this role. A B.Sc. in Hospitality and Hotel Administration offers broad operational and supervisory preparation, including extensive Food & Beverage Service laboratory training. NCHMCT describes the course as a three-year, six-semester programme; participating-institute admission is based on NCHM JEE rank and central counselling.

A BBA in Hotel Management is another broad hospitality-management route. For example, the University of Jammu publishes a BBA (Hotel Management) syllabus through its School of Hospitality and Tourism Management. Prospective students should examine the current curriculum, practical exposure, placement support, eligibility and admission process at each university rather than treating programme names as identical across institutions.

For a more directly service-focused pathway, NCHMCT’s Diploma in Food & Beverage Service covers food service, beverage service, food-and-beverage control, hygiene and sanitation, business communication, practical work and industrial training. NCHMCT states that the diploma is open to 10+2/equivalent applicants with English and is structured as 36 weeks of course work followed by 24 weeks of industrial training.
bullets: B.Sc. in Hospitality and Hotel Administration: broad hotel-operations and management foundation with Food & Beverage Service training.
BBA in Hotel Management: institution-specific hospitality-management degree route; verify the current university syllabus and admission rules.
Diploma in Hotel Management / Food & Beverage Service: shorter, service-specialist preparation with practical and industrial training.
Entry-level skill training can also support entry: THSC’s Food and Beverage Service Assistant qualification is at NSQF/NCrF Level 3 and lists Grade 10 pass as one entry option for that qualification.

sections.heading: Skills to build
paragraphs: Service competence combines guest-facing behaviour with disciplined operational execution. The NCHMCT B.Sc. curriculum identifies practical capabilities such as recognising food-and-beverage equipment, understanding service styles, greeting and seating guests, preparing mise-en-place, laying tables, and distinguishing à la carte from table d’hôte service. It also covers menu knowledge and basic food-and-beverage terminology.

Communication, etiquette, teamwork, hygiene and safety are equally important. THSC’s qualification framework explicitly includes effective communication, maintenance of service standards, customer privacy, professional etiquette, health and hygiene, safety procedures, waste-management practice, time use, attention to detail and coordination with colleagues and supervisors.
bullets: Guest communication, courteous greeting, service etiquette and complaint-escalation awareness.
Table set-up, mise-en-place, equipment handling, clearing and resetting procedures.
Menu, meal-period, service-style and beverage knowledge appropriate to the outlet.
Coordination with kitchen, stewarding, supervisors and fellow servers.
Personal grooming, sanitation, food-safety awareness, workplace safety and guest-data privacy.
Basic literacy, numeracy and accurate handling of routine service information.

sections.heading: Where and how the work is performed
paragraphs: The work is predominantly on-site and guest-facing. Food-and-beverage service is delivered across hotel restaurants, all-day dining outlets, coffee shops, cafeterias, fast-food or quick-service outlets, grill rooms, banquets, bars and related outlets. Depending on the employer, service may also be provided through room service, events and outdoor catering, or transport and institutional catering operations.

The role commonly requires movement between dining areas, service stations and kitchen pass areas; it also involves repeated set-up, replenishment, clearing and cleaning checks. Shift patterns and peak workloads are determined by meal periods, banquets, events and the outlet’s operating hours.
bullets: Hotels, resorts and stand-alone restaurants.
Banquet halls, event venues and outdoor catering operations.
Cafeterias, quick-service restaurants, cafés and bars.
Room service and specialised catering, including airline, hospital, cruise and railway contexts.

sections.heading: Career progression
paragraphs: A common progression is from entry service support or associate work toward supervision and outlet leadership. THSC’s published progression path moves from Food & Beverage Service Assistant to Food & Beverage Service Associate, Food & Beverage Supervisor, Restaurant Captain, Restaurant Manager, Manager–Banquet Operations and Food & Beverage Service Manager. This is a sector framework rather than a guaranteed employer ladder.

Progression is strengthened by reliable service execution, outlet and menu knowledge, ability to coordinate a service team, hygiene and safety discipline, and increasing exposure to banquet, bar, room-service or restaurant operations. The NCHMCT diploma curriculum also introduces food-and-beverage control topics such as cost, sales, purchasing, receiving, storage and menu analysis, which are relevant as responsibilities become more operationally supervisory.
bullets: Entry support / service assistant or associate roles.
Service executive, senior server, captain or shift-supervision roles, depending on the employer’s title structure.
Restaurant, banquet or outlet management roles.
Food-and-beverage management, including broader resource, quality and service oversight.
